Catalog Description

Operating Systems. (3-0). Credit 3. Hardware/software evolution leading to

contemporary operating systems; basic operating systems concepts; methods of

operating systems design and construction including algorithms for CPU

scheduling, memory and general resource allocation, process coordination and

management; case studies of several operating systems. Prerequisites: Grade of C

or better in CSCE 313; grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 313; grade of C or better

in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331.

Course Learning Outcomes

At the end of the course, the students will be able to:

Describe and compare the role, the architecture, and the major components of an

operating system.

Describe the role and operation of exception and interrupt handling in modern

operating systems.

Implement components of a virtual memory manager for a paged system.

Evaluate and compare various forms of concurrency mechanisms.

Evaluate and compare and apply various synchronization mechanisms, both for

single-processor and multiprocessor systems.

Implement components of a threading system for a single-processor system.

Implement components of a simple device driver.

Implement components of a simple file system.

Evaluate and compare the various virtualization approaches and their effect on

system performance.

Evaluate and compare and apply various synchronization mechanisms, both for

single-processor and multiprocessor systems.

Implement components of a threading system for a single-processor system.

Implement components of a simple device driver.

Implement components of a simple file system.

Evaluate and compare the various virtualization approaches and their effect on

system performance.

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

Advanced Programming in the UNIX Environment

ISBN:  978-0321637734

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Submission of Work, Deadline Policy, and Late Submission

Late assignments are not accepted.

The lab is due by 11:59 PM on the due date.

Timed assignments must be submitted within the allocated time.

Email submissions are not accepted.

If you have difficulties finishing an assignment, contact the instructor  before

the deadline.

One-time pass

You are eligible to receive a  one-time  waiver for one late  programming

assignment .

You can only use the pass  once .

The one-time pass gives you a 24-hour extension past the due date.

A team (2 students) is eligible for two one-time passes. You can use each pass

for different labs, or stack them for the same lab.

Fill out  this form  to submit a late lab within 24 hours after the due date.
